column44: Provides a summary of the action, including its purpose, scope, and goals, outlining the strategic approach to enhancing organizational quality.

column21:
Instructions
The Quality Action DocType is designed to streamline the management of quality improvement processes within the institution, focusing on problem resolution, performance monitoring, and continual improvement. This system facilitates the documentation, tracking, and analysis of actions taken to enhance operational excellence and compliance.


    
        Tab Name
        Description
    
    
        🎯 Resolution
        Focuses on identifying and documenting problems and actions taken, detailing responsibilities and tracking the status of each action for clear accountability.
    
    
        🚀 Performance and Evaluation
        Includes monitoring of performance indicators and evaluation criteria to assess the impact and effectiveness of actions taken, guiding future quality improvements.
    
    
        🚧 Risk and Resources
        Details the identification of potential risks and opportunities, and outlines the resources required to support quality initiatives, ensuring preparedness and strategic resource allocation.
    
    
        👾 Documentation
        Manages the storage and retrieval of all documentation related to quality actions, facilitating easy access to historical data and compliance records.
    


Utilize this DocType to enhance your quality management processes, ensuring robust documentation, effective resolution, and continual organizational improvement.

column21:
Quality & Innovation Performance Index (QIPI)
Definition: A composite score representing the average normalised change across all performance indicators—i.e., your combined measure of quality actions and innovation impact. 
 
Formula: Quality & Innovation Performance (QIPI) = Sum of Normalised Change Outcomes (%) ÷ Number of Indicators
 
How to Read It: 

    Higher QIPI: Greater overall performance improvement in both quality and innovation.
    Lower QIPI: Smaller or less impactful changes.

 
Description

    Perspective: Represents the high-level category or area being assessed (e.g., Internal Processes, Financial, CSR Impact). This groups performance indicators into overarching themes for easier interpretation.
    BSC Criteria: Indicates the specific metric or focus area being evaluated within the perspective (e.g., Resource Allocation, Process Innovation). These criteria align with broader organisational goals.
    UOM (Unit of Measure): The unit used to measure the indicator (e.g., minutes, percentage, numbers). It specifies how the before and after values are quantified.
    Before:  The baseline value recorded before the quality action or initiative was implemented. This serves as a reference point for comparison.
    After: The value recorded after the quality action or initiative was implemented. This indicates the result or impact of the initiative.
    Min Value: The smallest possible or relevant value for this indicator within the current context. Used as part of the normalisation process to standardise values across different scales.
    Max Value: The largest possible or relevant value for this indicator within the current context. Also used in the normalisation process to ensure consistency.
    Diff (%) (Automatically Calculated)
        
            Formula: ((After - Before) / Before) × 100
            Positive Number: Indicates an increase in the value from before to after. Whether this is favourable or unfavourable depends on the context (e.g., higher revenue is good, but higher error rates are bad).
            Negative Number: Indicates a decrease in the value from before to after. Similarly, the desirability depends on the context (e.g., lower processing time is good, but lower customer satisfaction is bad).
            Interpreting Smaller vs Bigger: Smaller values are better when reducing inefficiencies (e.g., processing time), while bigger values are better when achieving growth or positive improvements (e.g., satisfaction scores).
        
    
    Norm Diff (%) (Full Name: Normalised Difference)
        
            Formula: |((After - Before) / (Max Value - Min Value)) × 100|
            The value is always converted to positive (absolute) because the aim is to compare the magnitude of the impact, regardless of the direction.
            Why Positive? This ensures consistency in aggregating results, as some improvements naturally result in negative changes (e.g., reduced time) while others result in positive changes (e.g., increased revenue).
            Interpreting Smaller vs Bigger: A bigger value represents a larger impact relative to the defined range (Min Value to Max Value). Smaller values indicate less significant change or improvement.
        
    

 
Summary of Reading the Table:

    Use the Diff (%) to understand directional change (positive or negative).
    Refer to Norm Diff (%) to assess the scale of the impact across all indicators in a standardised way.
    Context matters: Always consider whether the performance indicator benefits from an increase or decrease. For example:
            Time savings → Smaller is better (e.g., reduced processing time).
            Revenue growth → Bigger is better (e.g., increased earnings).
        
    

 
For more information refer to: Quality & Innovation Performance Index

Aggregated Performance Index (API):

    Definition:
        
            A composite score representing the average normalised change across all performance indicators.
            Formula:API = Sum of Normalised Change Outcomes (%) ÷ Number of Indicators
        
    
    How to Read API:
        
            Higher API: Indicates significant improvements across performance indicators. Bigger is better, as it reflects higher performance improvements.
            Lower API: Suggests minimal changes or less impactful actions.
        
    
    Use Case:
        
            Provides a general overview of performance improvements.
            Helps identify whether quality actions are leading to meaningful and measurable changes.

column44: Calculator to calculate People (Man hour)
Operating cost before the improvement
Specifies how many times this activity occurs in one month, used to estimate the frequency of cost or time savings.
Time taken before improvement (1 man-day = 8 working hours)
Automatically calculates the annual frequency by multiplying Cycle per Month by 12 to support yearly performance reporting.
Standardised staff cost per man-day (e.g. 280)
Represents the total annual operating cost before improvement, calculated as People Cost Before per cycle multiplied by Total Cycles per Year.
Time taken after improvement (1 man-day = 8 working hours)
Represents the total annual operating cost after improvement, calculated as People Cost After per cycle multiplied by Total Cycles per Year.
Operating cost after the improvement
Represents the total annualised cost-time efficiency gain, calculated as Annual People Cost Before minus Annual People Cost After.

column18:
CTEI Supporting Narrative – Instructions for Completion



  Please complete this section to justify and contextualise the cost-time savings reported in the CTEI index. Your response should be factual, concise, and verifiable. Using the suggested structure below:



  
    Reason for Cost/Time Change
    • Describe what operational or procurement change led to the reduction in cost or time
    • Confirm that no costs were offloaded to another department, phase, or hidden line item
    • Mention whether the change was initiated by staff feedback, audit finding, or strategic plan
  
  
    Recurrence
    • Clarify if the saving is a one-time gain, a recurring monthly/quarterly/annual benefit, or part of a phased implementation
    • If phased, indicate expected cumulative value over time
    • State whether the saving applies to all future cohorts, processes, or just this instance
  
  
    Quality Impact Check
    • Confirm whether service quality, compliance, or learner satisfaction was preserved or improved
    • Include any related student/staff feedback, turnaround time, error reduction, or KPI movement
    • Mention any controls or monitoring in place to ensure quality isn’t compromised in future
  
  
    Evidence on File
    • List supporting documents: invoice numbers, budget approvals, quotations, contract changes, meeting minutes
    • Confirm where evidence is stored (ERPNext, Drive, File Manager, physical folder, etc.)
    • Optional: note who verified the data (e.g. Finance, Procurement, QA)
  
  
    Risks or Assumptions (Optional)
    • Disclose any risks, such as reliance on external vendors, variable exchange rates, or short-term discounts
    • Mention any assumptions used in the calculation (e.g. fixed man-day rate, full utilisation)
    • Flag if there are dependencies (e.g. training must be completed before time saving is realised)
  
  
    Additional Notes (Optional)
    • Use this to add clarification, disclaimers, or future plans (e.g. “A follow-up review will be done in 3 months”)
    • Any relevant internal reference codes or project IDs

column21:
CTEI – Cost-Time Efficiency Index
    CTEI measures the financial value of efficiency improvements achieved through operational changes, digitalisation, or innovation initiatives by converting time savings (expressed in man-days, where 1 man-day = 8 working hours) and cost reductions into Singapore dollar value (SGD).


     


    Formula:
    CTEI Savings = ((Before Time − After Time) × Man-Day Rate) + (Before Cost − After Cost)
     


    Definitions:


    
        Before Cost (SGD): Operating cost before improvement
    
    
        Before Time (Man-Day): Time taken before the improvement, expressed in man-days (1 man-day = 8 working hours)
    
    
        After Cost (SGD): Operating cost after the improvement
    
    
        After Time (Man-Day): Time taken after the improvement, in man-days (1 man-day = 8 working hours)
    
    
        Man-Day Rate: Fixed staff cost per man-day (e.g. SGD 280/day)
    
    
        CTEI Savings (SGD): Total cost-time efficiency gain in Singapore dollars
    


     


    Output Unit:
    SGD ($) – Total quantified value of cost/time efficiency gained.


     


    Compliance Relevance:
    Aligns with EduTrust 6.3.1 Innovation and Continual Improvement /7.23 Operational Outcomes and ISO 9001:2015 (Clauses 8.5.1, 9.1.3, 10.3) by providing auditable evidence of continual improvement, strategic investment, and resource optimisation.

column18:
When filling out the performance indicators' notes, focus on qualitative impacts rather than quantitative details, as quantitative metrics are already provided in the performance indicator table above. Please include the following aspects in your feedback:

    Contextual Impact: Describe how the changes have influenced daily operations and decision-making within the organization.
    Cultural Shifts: Highlight any shifts in organizational culture, such as improved collaboration or openness to innovation.
    Strategic Alignment: Discuss how the improvements align with broader organizational goals and compliance standards.
    Employee Engagement: Reflect on changes in employee engagement or satisfaction due to the implemented actions.
     

Use the following criteria to justify and assess the Qualitative ROE (Return on Expectations):

    Stakeholder Satisfaction: Enhances stakeholder experiences through improved satisfaction scores, streamlined service delivery, or positive feedback.
    Ease of Use: Simplifies processes, tools, or workflows, reducing friction for users and improving efficiency.
    Risk Mitigation: Reduces compliance risks, operational disruptions, or reputational threats, ensuring resilience and regulatory alignment.
    Process Improvement: Streamlines workflows, reduces errors, and increases transparency for better operational outcomes.
    Innovation Enablement: Supports scalability, introduces new features, and facilitates growth, enhancing adaptability and competitiveness.
    Alignment with Strategic Goals: Contributes directly to institutional goals, such as operational excellence, knowledge management, or strategic growth.
    Learning and Development: Enhances staff skills and competencies, fostering continuous improvement and professional growth.
    Cultural Alignment: Reinforces organisational values and vision, aligning actions with strategic objectives.
    Sustainability Impact: Advances environmental goals through initiatives like waste reduction, energy efficiency, or sustainability practices.
    Community Engagement: Strengthens societal impact through CSR activities, improved educational accessibility, or community partnerships.
    Governance and Compliance: Strengthens governance frameworks and enhances compliance with standards, accreditations, and legal requirements.
    Employee Engagement: Boosts morale, satisfaction, and retention, creating a motivated and collaborative workforce.
